title: Visual Measurement from PDF Drawings — How to Quantify

data.overview: This section explains HOW to extract quantities from a PDF drawing image. The AI can see the drawing — it must use the visual information to measure lengths, count items, and identify components. Recognising an item is not enough — you MUST quantify it.
data.critical_rule: EVERY identified service must have a QUANTITY. If you can see ductwork on the drawing, you MUST provide a length in metres. If you can see grilles, you MUST provide a count. 'Identified but not quantified' is a FAILURE — the estimator cannot price items without quantities.

ductwork_measurement.title: How to Measure Ductwork Length from a Drawing Image
method: 1. FIND THE SCALE. Read the title block or scale bar. Typical scales: 1:50 (2cm = 1m) or 1:100 (1cm = 1m).
2. CALIBRATE. If a scale bar is visible, measure its pixel length and calculate your pixels-per-metre conversion. If grid lines are labelled (e.g., 7.5m between grids), use those as your reference.
3. TRACE EACH DUCT RUN. Starting from the AHU or main plant, follow each duct line to its end. Duct appears as two parallel lines (rectangular) or a single line with Ø annotation (circular).
4. MEASURE EACH SEGMENT. For each straight segment between fittings (bends, tees, reducers), estimate the length by comparing it to your scale reference. Measure along the centreline.
5. SUM BY SIZE. Group all segments of the same duct size together. Report: 'Ø250mm circular duct — 45m' not just 'ductwork present'.
6. COUNT FITTINGS. At every direction change, count a bend. At every branch, count a tee. At every size change, count a reducer.
ductwork_measurement.common_failure: The AI identifies '250mm circular supply ductwork' but reports 'quantity_estimated: 45' as a guess instead of measuring from the drawing. This is WRONG. You must trace each run and measure using the scale.

measurement_example.drawing: 1:50 scale plan showing supply ductwork from AHU to 9 grilles
measurement_example.method: AHU to first tee: ~6m. Tee to second tee: ~4m. Each branch to grille: ~2-3m average. 9 branches × 2.5m = 22.5m + 10m mains = ~32.5m total.
measurement_example.output: 250mmØ circular supply duct — 33m (measured from 1:50 plan, cross-validated against grid spacing)

ductwork_measurement.if_scale_unknown: If you cannot determine the scale, estimate using room dimensions (typical office room 4-6m wide), door widths (900mm), or grid spacing. State your assumption. A rough measurement is better than no measurement.

counting_method.title: How to Count Items Systematically
method: 1. SCAN LEFT TO RIGHT, then TOP TO BOTTOM. Cover every part of the drawing systematically.
2. For each item type (grilles, VCDs, dampers), count ALL instances across the full drawing.
3. TRACE DUCTWORK LEGS. Each duct leg typically terminates at a grille/diffuser. The number of duct legs = number of terminal devices.
4. CHECK YOUR COUNT. If you counted 7 supply grilles but there are 9 duct branches, you missed 2. Recount.
5. DISTINGUISH SUPPLY FROM RETURN. Supply grilles have arrows pointing OUT of the grille (into the room). Return grilles have arrows pointing IN (back toward the duct).
6. VCDs: There is typically one VCD per duct branch (one per grille/terminal). Count VCDs by counting duct legs.
common_errors: Counting supply and return grilles as the same type — they are separate items
Missing grilles in corners or behind equipment — scan the entire drawing
Confusing VCD symbols with other damper types — check the legend
Missing items where services overlap — trace each duct leg individually

pipework_measurement.title: How to Measure Pipework Length from a Drawing Image
method: 1. Identify pipe runs by their line type (solid = this level, dashed = above/below).
2. Measure each segment against the scale. Pipes are single lines with diameter annotations.
3. For flow and return pipes running in parallel, measure ONE and double it (flow ≈ return).
4. Include vertical drops from tray to equipment (typically add 1-2m per drop if not dimensioned).

output_requirements.rule: For every item in your extraction output, you MUST provide:
fields: description — what the item is (e.g., '250mmØ circular supply ductwork')
quantity — a NUMBER with a UNIT (e.g., '33' with unit 'm', or '9' with unit 'nr')
unit — 'm' for linear items, 'nr' for counted items, 'm²' for area items
source — 'measured' if measured from drawing, 'counted' if counted from drawing, 'estimated' if derived
confidence — High/Medium/Low based on measurement certainty
never_acceptable: 'quantity_estimated: 45' with no explanation of how 45 was derived
'Multiple runs visible' — this is NOT a quantity
'Ductwork present throughout ground floor' — WHERE is the length?
'Approximately 12 supply grilles' — count them exactly, don't approximate
Leaving quantity as 0 or null for an item you can clearly see on the drawing

refrigerant_pipework.rule: Refrigerant pipe is shown as dashed lines on AC drawings per the legend. Measure from outdoor unit to each indoor unit. Count as TWO pipes (liquid + suction) — measure the route once, report as paired pipes.
refrigerant_pipework.measurement: Trace the dashed pipe route from BC-01 (or condenser) to each AC unit. Sum all routes. If outdoor unit location is off-drawing, measure to the drawing edge and note 'continues beyond drawing extent'.
refrigerant_pipework.if_not_visible: If refrigerant pipework is not visually distinguishable from other services, flag as [UNABLE TO TRACE — requires legend or colour differentiation].
